transcript.presenting_concern: SSRI augmentation with trazodone for sleep, PHQ-9 review

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Diane, how are you doing today? Come on in, sit down. Let me pull up your chart — I see we got your bloodwork back from last week.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'm... hanging in there. Barely, some days, but hanging in there. The sleep is still terrible. I'm taking the trazodone like you said but I still wake up at 3 AM and can't get back to sleep. Then I'm dragging all day long.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Tell me more about that. What happens when you wake at 3 AM? Are you startled awake, or do you just drift into wakefulness?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I just... surface. It's like my brain clicks on. And once I'm awake, I start thinking. About Harold. About all the things I need to do but don't have the energy to do. The house needs painting — Harold always handled that. The gutters are full of leaves from last fall. And then I think about how I'm sixty-three years old and living in this big house alone and what's the point of any of it. And then I'm wide awake and there's no going back to sleep.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the waking is followed by rumination — cycling thoughts about loss, the house, existential questions. That's very typical of depressive insomnia. The brain wakes up and immediately finds the pain. What time are you finally getting up?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Usually about 5:30. I lie there from 3 until 5:30 just staring at the ceiling. Sometimes I cry. Sometimes I just lie there feeling nothing. Then I get up and make coffee because there's nothing else to do.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. And what dose of trazodone are you on currently?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: 50mg at bedtime. It does help me fall asleep — I'm usually out by ten-thirty now, which is better than before when I was lying awake until midnight. But that 3 AM waking is like clockwork. My eyes open and I know it's 3 AM before I even look at the clock.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep is what we call "terminal insomnia" — it's a classic biological marker of depression, actually separate from the initial insomnia. The fact that trazodone handles the initial falling asleep but not the maintenance through the night tells me a couple of things. One, the dose may be too low — trazodone has a relatively short half-life of about five to nine hours, and at 50mg, you might be losing pharmacological coverage by 3 AM. Two, the underlying depression is driving the early waking regardless of the sleep medication.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So it's not just that the medication isn't working — it's that the depression itself is waking me up?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Think of it this way: the trazodone is doing its job getting you to sleep. But the depression is like an alarm clock set for 3 AM that goes off regardless. We need to address both the sleep aid dose and the overall depression management. Let me ask about the sertraline. What dose are you on now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: 100mg. I've been on that for... three years now? Dr. Morrison started it about four months after Harold passed. I remember because I resisted it for those first four months — kept thinking the grief would just pass on its own, that it was normal to feel that way. But it kept getting worse instead of better.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And when the sertraline started, did it help?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Oh yes. Significantly. Within about six weeks, I felt like the fog was lifting. I started going out again, calling my friends. I wouldn't say I was happy — Harold was still gone — but I was functioning. I could get through a day without crying. I could read a book. I could have lunch with my daughter and actually enjoy it instead of just going through the motions.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So it worked well initially, and for quite some time. When did you start noticing things slipping — feeling worse again?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Probably six months ago. Gradually. It wasn't like one day I was fine and the next I wasn't. It was more like... the volume on everything got slowly turned down. Colors getting dimmer. Food tasting like nothing. I stopped calling my friends. Stopped going to book club — I couldn't concentrate on reading anyway. Stopped cooking real meals. I'm living on toast, soup from a can, maybe a frozen dinner if I have the energy to microwave it. My daughter Jennifer noticed and started calling every day to check on me. I could hear the worry in her voice and that made me feel worse — like I'm a burden now.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That gradual withdrawal and loss of interest is concerning, and it sounds like it represents a recurrence of the depressive episode despite the sertraline. This happens — sometimes an antidepressant works well for a period and then what we call "tachyphylaxis" occurs, where the brain adapts to the medication and it loses effectiveness. It doesn't mean the medication failed; it means we need to adjust our approach. Diane, let me go through the PHQ-9 with you — our standard depression screening. I'd like to take it item by item so we can really discuss each one.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Alright.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Over the past two weeks, how often have you had little interest or pleasure in doing things? We're using: not at all, several days, more than half the days, or nearly every day.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. I used to love gardening — Harold and I had the most beautiful garden. I haven't been out there since last summer. I can see it from the kitchen window, all overgrown. The roses Harold planted need pruning. I think about doing it and I just... can't. There's no spark. No wanting.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's anhedonia — the loss of pleasure. It's one of the core symptoms. And the garden being connected to Harold adds a grief layer. Next item: feeling down, depressed, or hopeless?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More than half the days. I'd say four or five days a week, I have this heavy, gray feeling. Not every single day — Sundays when Jennifer brings the grandkids over, I perk up a little. But it comes right back after they leave. The house gets so quiet.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So there's some mood reactivity — you can brighten in response to positive events. That's actually a helpful sign. It tells me the depression hasn't become completely fixed. Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day — the 3 AM problem we discussed. Every single night.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Feeling tired or having little energy?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. I feel like I'm walking through molasses. Simple things — taking a shower, getting dressed, driving to the grocery store — they feel like climbing a mountain. I've been wearing the same cardigan for three days because the thought of choosing another one is exhausting. That's not like me. I used to be put-together. Harold used to say I was the most elegant woman in any room.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I can see this isn't who you know yourself to be. Poor appetite or overeating?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Poor appetite. More than half the days. I forget to eat and then I'm lightheaded by 2 PM and realize I haven't eaten anything all day. The toast and soup I mentioned — that's not because I want toast and soup. It's because that's all I can manage to prepare when I can barely stand at the counter long enough to put bread in the toaster.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Have you lost weight?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: About eight pounds since Christmas. My clothes are loose. Jennifer noticed and she keeps bringing me casseroles. I eat them to make her feel better but I can't taste anything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Eight pounds unintentional weight loss is significant. We'll note that. Feeling bad about yourself — or that you are a failure or have let yourself or your family down?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Several days. I feel like... like I've become this pathetic old woman who can't manage her own life. Harold handled so much — the finances, the house maintenance, the car. And now I'm supposed to do all of that alone and I can't even get out of bed some mornings. I feel like I should be further along. It's been three years. People expect you to "move on" by now.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There's no timeline on grief, Diane. And the depression is separate from the grief — it's a complication of it, not the same thing. Trouble concentrating on things, such as reading or watching television?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More than half the days. I used to be an avid reader — three books a month for our book club. Now I can't get through a page without my mind drifting. I'll reread the same paragraph five times and still not know what it said. I've been watching television but I couldn't tell you what shows. It's just noise to fill the quiet.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed? Or being fidgety and restless?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The slow one. Jennifer says I'm moving like I'm underwater. Even my voice feels slower. Like it takes effort to form words.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I notice that too — your speech is slower today than when I first met you. That's psychomotor retardation and it's a biological sign of depression. Last question — and I ask everyone this, Diane. Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Not... actively. But I've had moments where I think "if I didn't wake up tomorrow, that would be okay." Not that I want to DO anything. But if God decided to take me in my sleep... I wouldn't mind. I'd be with Harold. Is that bad?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It's not "bad" — it's passive suicidal ideation, and it's important that you told me. You're not making plans or intending to act, but you're expressing a willingness to die. That tells me the depression is serious and we need to be more aggressive in treating it. I'm going to score that as "several days." Is that accurate?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. It's not every day. Just... the really hard days. The 3 AM days when the house is dark and empty and I wonder why I'm still here without him.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you for telling me, Diane. We'll make sure to address this. Your total score today is 17, which is in the moderately severe range. When we last did this three months ago, you were at 12 — the moderate range. So we've seen an increase of five points. That tells me clearly that the sertraline at 100mg is no longer providing adequate coverage. We need to change our approach.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Before you go on — I want to ask about something. My friend Ruth is on Wellbutrin and she says it gave her energy. She says she went from not being able to get off the couch to cleaning her whole house in a day. I feel like my biggest problem is that I have no energy or motivation. Everything feels like it takes so much effort. Would Wellbutrin help with that specifically?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good question, and Ruth's experience is actually pretty typical. Bupropion — the generic name for Wellbutrin — works on different brain chemicals than sertraline. Sertraline is a serotonin reuptake inhibitor — it boosts serotonin. Bupropion works on norepinephrine and dopamine, which are more involved in energy, motivation, pleasure, and drive. That's why it has that activating, energizing profile that's different from what you're getting with sertraline alone.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So it targets the exact things I'm struggling with most — the energy and the motivation.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Precisely. Now, there are two approaches. We could switch from sertraline to bupropion entirely, or we could add bupropion to your sertraline as an augmentation strategy. I'm going to recommend the augmentation approach — keeping both — for several reasons. The sertraline is still providing some benefit. It has anxiolytic properties — it reduces anxiety and worry — and it supports sleep. If we pulled it entirely and only used bupropion, you might feel more energized but also more anxious, and your sleep could worsen. The augmentation approach — using both together — is actually very well-studied for what we call treatment-resistant depression. The combination targets different neurotransmitter systems and often produces better results than either medication alone.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Would there be interactions between them? I'm already on so many pills — the sertraline, the trazodone, my blood pressure medication, the statin. I feel like I'm rattling when I walk.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [smiles] I understand that concern. Let me review your full medication list and check interactions. You're on sertraline 100mg, trazodone 50mg, lisinopril 10mg for blood pressure, and atorvastatin 20mg for cholesterol. No significant interactions between bupropion and any of those. There is a minor pharmacokinetic interaction between sertraline and bupropion — sertraline can slightly increase bupropion levels — but it's clinically manageable at the doses we'd be using and actually can be beneficial.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: What about side effects? Ruth mentioned she had some headaches starting it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The most common side effects with bupropion are headache, dry mouth, mild nausea, and sometimes difficulty sleeping — which is why we always dose it in the morning, never at night. Most side effects are mild and resolve within the first week or two as your body adjusts. The main serious consideration is that bupropion lowers the seizure threshold — which means if someone has a seizure history, we'd be cautious. Do you have any seizure history?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No, no seizures ever.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. And no history of eating disorders? Bupropion isn't recommended for people with current anorexia or bulimia because of the seizure risk at very low body weight.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No, nothing like that. I'm not eating enough right now but that's the depression, not an eating disorder.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Understood, and your current weight loss is something we'll monitor. One more benefit I should mention — bupropion is weight-neutral to slightly weight-promoting in terms of appetite, which could actually help your appetite situation. Some patients report food starts tasting better and they feel more interested in eating.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Well, that would be welcome. I miss enjoying food. Harold and I used to cook together every Sunday. Italian, mostly. His nonna's recipes.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Maybe that's something to work toward as the medication kicks in. So here's my plan: I'm going to start you on bupropion XL — that's the extended-release form, one pill in the morning — at 150mg. That's the starting dose. You should notice some increased energy within the first one to two weeks. The full antidepressant effect takes four to six weeks. If at our next visit you're tolerating it well but still not where we want you, we can increase to 300mg. In the meantime, sertraline stays at 100mg.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: And the trazodone for sleep?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's increase that to 75mg. The additional 25mg should extend the coverage later into the night. If you're still waking at 3 AM after a week at 75mg, we'll move to 100mg. I don't want to go higher than 100mg without seeing you again first. Take it about thirty minutes before your intended bedtime — so if you want to be asleep by 10:30, take it at 10:00.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Got it. So to make sure I have this right: sertraline stays at 100 in the morning, add the Wellbutrin 150 also in the morning, and trazodone goes up to 75 at night.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Correct. Take the sertraline and bupropion together with breakfast. They're fine to take at the same time. Now — your labs. I want to go over these with you because there are a few things relevant to how you're feeling. The good news: thyroid function is completely normal — TSH of 2.1, well within range. Hypothyroidism can cause fatigue and depression, so ruling that out is important. B12 is normal at 450. Kidney and liver function look fine — this is important because it tells me your body is metabolizing the medications well.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's reassuring at least.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: However — your vitamin D level is low at 18 nanograms per milliliter. We like to see it above 30, ideally above 40. Vitamin D deficiency is very common in older adults, especially those who aren't getting outside much, and it can contribute significantly to fatigue, low mood, muscle weakness, and even cognitive fog. It's not causing your depression by itself, but it's making everything worse.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I haven't been going outside at all. The garden, the walks — I've stopped all of it. So that makes sense.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I want to start you on vitamin D3, 2000 IU daily. Take it with a meal that has some fat — it absorbs better with food. We'll recheck in three months and adjust if needed.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My daughter Jennifer has been telling me to take vitamin D for two years. She'll be pleased.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Daughters are often right. [both laugh] They pay more attention to these things than we give them credit for. Now Diane, I want to check in beyond the medication. This isn't just about pills — there's a behavioral component that's equally important. Are you getting outside at all? Moving your body?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not much. Honestly, barely at all. I go to the grocery store once a week — sometimes not even that; Jennifer does it for me now. I used to walk every morning — Harold and I walked two miles around the neighborhood every day before breakfast. After he died, I kept it up for maybe six months. Then I stopped. It felt too sad to walk that route alone, passing the neighbors who'd wave and look at me with pity. "Poor Diane, walking alone now."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I understand that. Those routines we share with people become painful reminders — the empty space beside you where someone used to be. And the neighbors' reactions added a social discomfort on top of the grief. But Diane, physical activity is a significant piece of depression management. The research shows that regular moderate exercise — even just walking — can be comparable to medication in its antidepressant effects. It increases brain-derived neurotrophic factor, releases endorphins, improves sleep, increases energy. It's not a replacement for medication in your case, but it's a powerful addition.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I know I should walk. I know it. But the getting-started part... when I can barely get dressed...

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear you. And I'm not asking you to recreate the routine you had with Harold. That belonged to you and him. What if you created something new — a different route, a different time of day, different company? The senior center — do they still have that morning walking group?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: They do. Ruth goes. She's asked me to join maybe ten times. I always say "maybe next week" and then next week comes and I don't go.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What stops you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] The effort. Getting up, getting dressed, driving there, making small talk with people. And honestly... a fear that I'll get there and start crying and embarrass myself. I cry so easily now. Anything can set me off.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those are both depression talking. The low energy makes everything feel Herculean, and the emotional lability makes you want to hide from others. But here's what I want you to consider: the women in that walking group are your age. Many of them have lost partners. They would understand tears. And the act of going — of putting one foot in front of the other even when you don't want to — is what we call behavioral activation. You don't wait for motivation to arrive and then act. You act first, and the motivation and positive feelings follow the behavior.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "Motivation follows action." You've said that before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Because it's one of the most important truths in depression treatment. Depression tells you to wait until you feel like doing something. But if you wait for the feeling, you'll wait forever — because inactivity feeds the depression, which reduces motivation further. It's a downward spiral. Action breaks the spiral.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Harold always said something similar. He'd say "you'll never regret going to the thing — you only regret not going." Every time I didn't want to go somewhere and he convinced me to go, he was right. I always felt better after.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Smart man. He'd want you to go.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [tearing up] He would. He'd be so upset to see me like this. Living on toast. Not leaving the house. He'd say "Diane Marie Russell, get your shoes on and go."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Then let's honor that. Can you commit to joining Ruth one time this week? Not every day, not three times. Once. Just go once and see how it feels. If it's terrible, you never have to go back. But try it once.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [wiping eyes] Okay. I'll call her tonight and tell her I'm coming Tuesday. If I tell her, then I have to go. She won't let me back out — she's very persistent.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good — accountability helps. Use Ruth's persistence as a support. And Diane, one more behavioral piece: is there one small pleasurable activity you could add back this week? Not the whole garden — just one thing that used to bring you joy.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [thinking] I could... maybe listen to music. Harold and I used to listen to jazz in the evenings. I haven't turned on the stereo since he passed. But maybe I could try. Just background music while I have my tea.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That sounds lovely. Music, tea, sitting in a comfortable chair. Small pleasures. Start there. You don't have to rebuild your whole life in a week. Just one walk with Ruth and one evening with jazz.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I can try that. Thank you, Dr. Patel.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Before we wrap up — I want to return to something you said earlier, about not minding if you didn't wake up. I want you to know that if those thoughts become more intense, or if you start thinking about actively ending your life, I need you to call our crisis line or 988. Do you have those numbers?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I have the card you gave me, yes. In my purse.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. And Jennifer is calling daily, which is a protective factor. I don't think you're at immediate risk, but with your score increasing and those passive thoughts present, I want to be sure we have safety measures in place. The bupropion should start helping within two weeks, and as your energy improves and you re-engage with activities and people, those hopeless thoughts should diminish. But if they get worse before they get better, I want to hear about it immediately.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I understand. I'll call if things get dark.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you. Alright — let me summarize our plan. Medication changes: sertraline continues 100mg morning, add bupropion XL 150mg morning, trazodone increases to 75mg at bedtime, add vitamin D3 2000 IU daily with food. Behavioral goals: walk with Ruth one time this week, listen to music one evening. I'll see you in three weeks to check how the bupropion is going. We'll repeat the PHQ-9 at that visit and compare. If you're tolerating the bupropion well but still scoring above 10 on the PHQ-9, we'll consider increasing to 300mg. Call us if you have any concerning side effects — increased anxiety, insomnia worsening, headaches that don't resolve, or any worsening of those passive thoughts we discussed.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Three weeks. I can do that. And I'll walk with Ruth Tuesday.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I believe you will. And Diane — I'm hopeful. The augmentation strategy works well for many patients in your situation. You responded to sertraline before, which tells me your brain is responsive to pharmacological treatment. Adding bupropion gives us a second mechanism. Between the medication adjustment, the vitamin D, and even small behavioral changes, I expect you'll start feeling shifts.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr. Patel. I'm ready to feel something again. It's been too long living in this gray fog. Harold wouldn't want this for me. My grandchildren deserve a grandmother who's present, not just existing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: They do. And you deserve a life that has color and pleasure in it again. We'll get you there. Take care, Diane. I'll see you in three weeks.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank you. I'll let you know how Tuesday goes.

goals: SSRI augmentation with trazodone for sleep, PHQ-9 review
